title: The ethics of inheriting an unfinished house
summary: Argue whether someone who inherits a half-built family home owes it to their parents — or to themselves — to complete it.
topic: Whether the child who inherits a half-built family house has a duty to complete it
prompt: Write a 350–500-word argumentative essay about whether a person who inherits an unfinished house from their parents — common in places such as Bucharest, Tirana, Naples, and Marrakech, where homes are built over decades — has a moral obligation to complete the original plan, or whether it is acceptable to demolish, redesign, sell, or simply live in it as it stands. Use a 'what' cleft, a concession ('Granted, …' or 'Admittedly, …'), at least one passive construction, and one participle clause. Anchor the argument in at least one specific city or family pattern. Take a position.
hintStarters: What an unfinished family house silently asks of the next generation is something rarely written into the…
Granted, the assumption that grown children should organise their lives around a building their parents started can shade into a kind of family obligation that…
On the outskirts of Bucharest, a one-storey concrete shell built by a returning émigré in 1991 still stands with its rebar visible, its second floor permanently…

hintVocab.meaningVi: thừa kế ngôi nhà gia đình

hintVocab.phrase: to be left in a half-finished state
hintVocab.meaningVi: bị bỏ ở trạng thái dở dang

hintVocab.phrase: to be saddled with an inherited project
hintVocab.meaningVi: bị áp đặt một dự án thừa kế
tái sử dụng một tài sản cho mục đích khác

hintVocab.phrase: the weight of someone else's vision
hintVocab.meaningVi: gánh nặng của tầm nhìn người khác

hintVocab.phrase: to be inherited along with the keys
hintVocab.meaningVi: được thừa kế cùng với chìa khóa
ngồi lại với quyết định (suy nghĩ kỹ)

hintVocab.phrase: to dishonour a lifetime of saving
hintVocab.meaningVi: phụ lòng cả một đời tiết kiệm

sampleText:
On the outskirts of Bucharest stands a one-storey concrete shell built by a man who emigrated to Italy in the 1980s and sent home, year after year, just enough money to add a wall, a window, a row of bricks. He died before the second floor was finished. The rebar is still visible against the sky. His daughter, who now lives in Berlin, owns it and does not quite know what to do with it.

This house is not unusual. Across cities shaped by emigration and slow construction — Bucharest, Tirana, Naples, Marrakech, certain suburbs of Manila — a generation that left, saved, and sent money home has produced thousands of buildings that exist in a permanent middle state. The question of what the inheriting child owes these houses is more interesting than the answers usually given.

What an unfinished family house silently asks of the next generation is something rarely written into the will. The will leaves a property. The unspoken expectation often leaves a project: complete what I started, in something like the shape I imagined, with the kind of seriousness I was not able to give it. That expectation can sit very heavily on a person who has built a life elsewhere, in a different language, with no particular interest in spending the next decade managing a building site in a country they visit twice a year.

Granted, the inherited house is not nothing. It represents savings, sacrifice, a vision held across decades, and very often the most concrete object — in both senses — that a parent ever made. To casually demolish or sell it is to overwrite a lifetime of work, and there are children who will, later, regret having moved too fast. Some pause, some respect, some careful consideration are owed.

Even so, the duty is not to complete the house exactly as planned. It is to make a decision about it with adequate care. What that decision looks like, in practice, varies: completing it, completing it differently, partially rebuilding, selling it to a family who will live in it, donating it, demolishing it where the structure is dangerous. None of these is automatically the right answer; all of them can be honourable if reached deliberately. What is not honourable is letting it sit, untouched, for another twenty years, as a way of avoiding the choice entirely.

Admittedly, a daughter in Berlin cannot always organise a decision quickly. Permits, distance, family disagreement, and grief can all slow things down legitimately. The question is not how fast she decides but whether she eventually does. The parent's gift was a house. The inheritance — what is being inherited along with the keys — is the obligation to take it seriously enough to choose.

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Ở ngoại ô Bucharest đứng một khung bê tông một tầng được xây bởi một người đàn ông đã di cư sang Ý vào những năm 1980

sampleAnnotations.phrase: sent home, year after year, just enough money to add a wall, a window, a row of bricks
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: gửi về nhà, năm này qua năm khác, vừa đủ tiền để thêm một bức tường, một cửa sổ, một dãy gạch

sampleAnnotations.phrase: His daughter, who now lives in Berlin, owns it and does not quite know what to do with it

sampleAnnotations.phrase: a generation that left, saved, and sent money home has produced thousands of buildings that exist in a permanent middle state

sampleAnnotations.phrase: What an unfinished family house silently asks of the next generation is something rarely written into the will

sampleAnnotations.phrase: That expectation can sit very heavily on a person who has built a life elsewhere
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Mong đợi đó có thể đè rất nặng lên một người đã xây dựng cuộc sống ở nơi khác

sampleAnnotations.phrase: It represents savings, sacrifice, a vision held across decades
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Nó đại diện cho tiết kiệm, hy sinh, một tầm nhìn được giữ trong nhiều thập kỷ

sampleAnnotations.phrase: To casually demolish or sell it is to overwrite a lifetime of work
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Phá dỡ hay bán nó một cách tùy tiện là viết đè lên cả một đời công sức

sampleAnnotations.phrase: Some pause, some respect, some careful consideration are owed
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Một sự dừng lại, một sự tôn trọng, một sự cân nhắc cẩn thận là điều cần có

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the duty is not to complete the house exactly as planned. It is to make a decision about it with adequate care

sampleAnnotations.phrase: What is not honourable is letting it sit, untouched, for another twenty years, as a way of avoiding the choice entirely

sampleAnnotations.phrase: Permits, distance, family disagreement, and grief can all slow things down legitimately

sampleAnnotations.phrase: The question is not how fast she decides but whether she eventually does

sampleAnnotations.phrase: The parent's gift was a house. The inheritance — what is being inherited along with the keys — is the obligation to take it seriously enough to choose

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the most concrete object — in both senses — that a parent ever made
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: vật cụ thể nhất — theo cả hai nghĩa — mà một bậc cha mẹ từng tạo ra

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to surface a silent expectation
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What an unfinished family house silently asks of the next generation is something rarely written into the will' dùng cấu trúc 'What … is …' để nhân hóa ngôi nhà và đưa một kỳ vọng không lời ra ánh sáng. Trạng từ 'silently' và 'rarely' tạo ra một sự đối lập giữa điều ngầm được yêu cầu và điều thực sự được ghi chép.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What an unfinished family house silently asks of the next generation is something rarely written into the will' uses a what-cleft to personify the house and surface an unspoken expectation. The adverbs 'silently' and 'rarely' set up the contrast between what is implicitly demanded and what is actually written down.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Reduced relative clause with passive participle for compressed history
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'a one-storey concrete shell built by a man who emigrated to Italy in the 1980s' dùng mệnh đề quan hệ rút gọn ở dạng bị động ('built by a man') kết hợp với mệnh đề quan hệ đầy đủ ('who emigrated to Italy in the 1980s'). Cấu trúc này nén toàn bộ lịch sử của một ngôi nhà và người xây nó vào một cụm danh từ duy nhất.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'a one-storey concrete shell built by a man who emigrated to Italy in the 1980s' uses a reduced passive relative ('built by a man') combined with a full relative clause ('who emigrated to Italy in the 1980s'). The structure compresses the full history of a building and its builder into a single noun phrase.

sampleTranslationVi:
Ở ngoại ô Bucharest đứng một khung bê tông một tầng được xây bởi một người đàn ông đã di cư sang Ý vào những năm 1980 và gửi về nhà, năm này qua năm khác, vừa đủ tiền để thêm một bức tường, một cửa sổ, một dãy gạch. Ông qua đời trước khi tầng hai hoàn thiện. Cốt thép vẫn còn nhìn thấy in trên nền trời. Con gái ông, hiện sống ở Berlin, sở hữu nó và không biết phải làm gì với nó.

Ngôi nhà này không phải là điều bất thường. Trên các thành phố được định hình bởi sự di cư và xây dựng chậm — Bucharest, Tirana, Naples, Marrakech, một số ngoại ô của Manila — một thế hệ đã ra đi, tiết kiệm, và gửi tiền về đã tạo ra hàng ngàn tòa nhà tồn tại ở trạng thái giữa chừng vĩnh viễn. Câu hỏi về việc đứa con thừa kế nợ gì cho những ngôi nhà này thú vị hơn so với các câu trả lời thường được đưa ra.

Điều mà một ngôi nhà gia đình chưa hoàn thiện âm thầm yêu cầu ở thế hệ tiếp theo là điều hiếm khi được viết vào di chúc. Di chúc để lại một tài sản. Mong đợi không lời thường để lại một dự án: hãy hoàn thành điều mà tôi đã bắt đầu, theo gần đúng hình dạng tôi đã hình dung, với loại nghiêm túc mà tôi đã không thể dành cho nó. Mong đợi đó có thể đè rất nặng lên một người đã xây dựng cuộc sống ở nơi khác, bằng một ngôn ngữ khác, không đặc biệt quan tâm đến việc dành thập kỷ tiếp theo quản lý một công trường ở một đất nước mà họ chỉ ghé thăm hai lần một năm.

Thừa nhận rằng, ngôi nhà thừa kế không phải là không có gì. Nó đại diện cho tiết kiệm, hy sinh, một tầm nhìn được giữ trong nhiều thập kỷ, và rất thường là vật cụ thể nhất — theo cả hai nghĩa — mà một bậc cha mẹ từng tạo ra. Phá dỡ hay bán nó một cách tùy tiện là viết đè lên cả một đời công sức, và có những đứa con sau này sẽ hối tiếc vì đã hành động quá nhanh. Một sự dừng lại, một sự tôn trọng, một sự cân nhắc cẩn thận là điều cần có.

Dẫu vậy, bổn phận không phải là hoàn thành ngôi nhà chính xác như đã lên kế hoạch. Đó là đưa ra một quyết định về nó với sự cẩn thận thích đáng. Quyết định đó trông như thế nào, trong thực tế, thay đổi: hoàn thành nó, hoàn thành theo cách khác, xây dựng lại một phần, bán cho một gia đình sẽ sống trong đó, hiến tặng, phá dỡ nơi kết cấu nguy hiểm. Không có cái nào tự động là câu trả lời đúng; tất cả đều có thể đáng kính nếu được đưa ra một cách có chủ ý. Điều không đáng kính là để nó nằm đó, không được động đến, thêm hai mươi năm nữa, như một cách tránh né hoàn toàn sự lựa chọn.

Thừa nhận rằng, một người con gái ở Berlin không phải lúc nào cũng có thể tổ chức một quyết định nhanh chóng. Giấy phép, khoảng cách, bất đồng gia đình, và nỗi đau buồn đều có thể làm chậm mọi thứ một cách chính đáng. Câu hỏi không phải là cô quyết định nhanh đến mức nào mà là cuối cùng cô có quyết định hay không. Món quà của cha mẹ là một ngôi nhà. Di sản — điều đang được thừa kế cùng với chìa khóa — là nghĩa vụ phải coi trọng nó đủ để lựa chọn.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to surface a silent expectation?
options: Rarely written into the will what an unfinished house silently asks the next generation is something.
What an unfinished family house silently asks of the next generation is something rarely written into the will.
The next generation silently asks what an unfinished family house is something rarely written into the will.
Silently asks an unfinished family house what of the next generation is something rarely written the will into.
mcQuestions.explanation: The cleft 'What … is …' places the writer's diagnosis ('something rarely written into the will') in the emphatic end position, contrasting silent demand with written record.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … is …' with the writer's evaluation placed after 'is'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'moral debt' mean in this essay?
options: An unpaid financial loan from a relative
An unwritten obligation that one feels one owes, based on what someone else has done or sacrificed
A penalty issued by a court for unethical behaviour
A debt that one owes to oneself for personal failure
mcQuestions.explanation: Moral debt is an obligation that is not legally enforceable but is felt as binding because of what another person has done — a sacrifice, an investment, a long-held vision.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'debt' as 'something owed' and 'moral' as 'felt rather than legally required'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'granted' correctly as a concession?
options: Granted, the inherited house is not nothing.
The inherited house granted nothing not is.
Not nothing is the inherited granted house.
Is the inherited house granted not nothing.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted,' followed by a complete clause cleanly grants a real point to the opposing view before the essay continues.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option places 'Granted,' before a full, grammatical sentence?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a passive participle within a reduced relative clause?
options: A one-storey concrete shell built by a man who emigrated to Italy in the 1980s.
Built by a man a one-storey concrete shell who emigrated to Italy in the 1980s.
Emigrated to Italy in the 1980s a one-storey concrete shell built by a man who.
A man built a one-storey concrete shell who emigrated to Italy in the 1980s.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'built by a man …' is a reduced passive relative clause modifying 'shell', which is then itself followed by a full relative clause ('who emigrated to Italy in the 1980s'). The stacking is dense and economical.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'built by …' immediately after the noun, followed by 'who emigrated …'.

options: A national economy primarily based on tourism
An economy in which a significant share of household income comes from family members working abroad and sending money home
An economy with strict limits on cross-border money transfers
A digital payment system used between banks
mcQuestions.explanation: Remittances are payments sent home by migrants; in a remittance economy these inflows support household construction, savings, and consumption — and produce, among other things, the half-built houses described in the essay.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'remittance' as 'money sent home' — what kind of economy depends on that?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best paraphrases 'to overwrite a lifetime of work'?
options: To replace someone's life's effort as if it had never happened
To rewrite a parent's autobiography in a different style
To complete a project that someone else began
To save over a computer file that contained important data
mcQuestions.explanation: The metaphor borrows from word processing: 'overwrite' is to delete by replacement. To overwrite a lifetime of work is to act as if that work had never been done — a forceful image for casual demolition.
mcQuestions.hint: What does it mean to 'overwrite' a file, applied to a person's life work?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to repurpose a property' mean?
options: To formally rename a property at the land registry
To use a property for a purpose different from the one it was originally designed for
To purchase a property for the second time
To prepare a property for sale to overseas buyers
mcQuestions.explanation: Repurposing means changing what something is used for — turning a half-built family home into a clinic, a workshop, a rental flat — without necessarily completing the original plan.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 're' + 'purpose' — what does that suggest about changing use?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a participle clause to compress a list of options?
options: Completing it differently, partially rebuilding, selling it to a family who will live in it, donating it, demolishing it where the structure is dangerous.
It differently completing partially rebuilding selling it to a family donating demolishing it dangerous where structure.
Where the structure is dangerous demolishing it donating it selling it to a family completing it differently partially rebuilding.
Selling it to a family completing it differently partially rebuilding donating it demolishing it dangerous structure where.
mcQuestions.explanation: Each option is a present-participle clause ('completing…', 'rebuilding…', 'selling…', 'donating…', 'demolishing…'), and the list functions as an extended parallel — a B2 compression strategy.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for a sequence of present-participle phrases all standing as parallel options.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals a return to the main argument after a concession?
options: For example,
Even so, the duty is not to complete the house exactly as planned. It is to make a decision about it with adequate care.
On the contrary, the house should never be discussed.
Furthermore, all houses must be demolished.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' acknowledges the conceded point (the house is not nothing) and pivots back to the main argument that the duty is to decide, not to complete.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase both accepts the concession and reasserts the writer's position?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's central position?
options: Inherited unfinished houses must always be completed exactly as the original builder planned.
Inherited unfinished houses should always be demolished immediately.
The duty is not to complete the house as planned but to make a deliberate, careful decision about it within a reasonable time.
Inherited unfinished houses should be donated to the local government in every case.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues that any of several outcomes — completion, redesign, sale, donation, demolition — can be honourable, provided the decision is reached deliberately and not avoided indefinitely.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at paragraphs five and six for the writer's clearest statement of duty.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains the most formal academic register?
options: Honestly inheriting an unfinished house is kind of a nightmare and people should just sell.
Half-built houses are basically a guilt trip from your parents, no question.
The inheritance of an unfinished structure imposes a procedural rather than a substantive duty on the heir — namely, to reach a deliberate decision within a reasonable timeframe.
Inherited houses are super annoying and people stress about them way too much.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C uses formal vocabulary ('procedural rather than substantive duty', 'deliberate decision', 'reasonable timeframe') and avoids contractions, slang, and informal intensifiers.

options: A legal obligation owed by a child to a parent's estate
A moral obligation that a child feels toward their parent, especially with regard to honouring their wishes
The financial responsibility of supporting one's children
A formal religious requirement of obedience to elders
mcQuestions.explanation: Filial duty is the felt moral obligation of a child toward their parent — distinct from legal duty, and central to the unspoken pressure the inherited house imposes.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'filial' as 'relating to a son or daughter'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a defining relative clause?
options: His daughter, who now lives in Berlin, owns it and does not quite know what to do with it.
Who now lives in Berlin his daughter owns it and does not quite know what to do with it.
Owns it his daughter who now lives in Berlin and does not quite know what to do with it.
To do with it his daughter who now lives in Berlin owns it not quite knows what.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'who now lives in Berlin' is a non-defining relative clause (set off by commas), providing extra information about the daughter without restricting which daughter is meant.
mcQuestions.hint: Note the comma structure: a non-defining relative clause is set off by commas on both sides.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'the most concrete object — in both senses — that a parent ever made' mean?
options: An object that was made of concrete and was also the most specific item in their estate
A literal concrete (cement) object that is also the most tangible, definite achievement of their life
A piece of art made from concrete that has artistic value
A specific piece of paperwork describing a concrete object
mcQuestions.explanation: The phrase plays on the double meaning of 'concrete': both the literal building material and the figurative sense of 'most definite, tangible, real'.
mcQuestions.hint: What are the two meanings of 'concrete' in English?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best closes the essay with a sharp distinction between gift and inheritance?
options: In conclusion, half-built houses are a complicated subject.
Therefore, all inherited houses must be demolished.
The parent's gift was a house. The inheritance — what is being inherited along with the keys — is the obligation to take it seriously enough to choose.
To sum up, family obligations and personal freedom both matter.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C distinguishes precisely between the gift (the building) and the inheritance (the duty to decide), closing the essay on its strongest distinction.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option ends with a sharp distinction stated in parallel sentences?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say is specifically NOT honourable?
options: Completing the house exactly as the parent planned
Selling the house to a family who will live in it
Letting the house sit untouched for another twenty years as a way of avoiding the choice entirely
Donating the house to charity
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says explicitly that the dishonourable response is indefinite avoidance — letting the house sit for decades as a way of never having to decide.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the end of paragraph five for the writer's named exception.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If a parent or grandparent of yours left you a project that they had not finished — a house, a manuscript, a business, a piece of land — what obligation would you actually feel? Would you complete it, change it, or let it go, and how long would you give yourself to decide?
